1: Precise dating: seventh year, fifth month, tenth day — about 591 BCE. anashim mizziqnei yisrael ('some of the elders of Israel'): zeqenim (H2205, elders) — the leadership delegation. lidrosh et-YHWH ('to inquire of/seek YHWH'): darash (H1875, inquire/seek/resort to) — the technical verb for prophetic consultation. YHWH refuses to be consulted by those who will not repent.
2: Standard messenger formula. The chapter that follows is Ezekiel's most extensive historical review — a covenant lawsuit (riv) tracing four generations of rebellion from Egypt to the present, in a structure that shows each generation receiving grace and abusing it, followed by divine restraint for the sake of the divine Name.
3: im-yiddaresh ani lachem ('I will not let myself be consulted by you'): the divine refusal to be 'inquired of' (darash) by those who continue in idolatry. This is not divine accessibility — it is divine integrity. YHWH does not provide oracular guidance while the inquirers maintain contradictory loyalties. The Niphal of darash here ('I will not be sought/consulted') is emphatic self-distancing.
4: hashophet otam ('will you judge them?'): YHWH asks Ezekiel to pronounce judgment by making known their fathers' abominations. The forensic vocabulary (shaphat, H8199, judge) positions the entire historical review as a legal indictment. hoda otam et-toavot avotam ('make known to them the abominations of their fathers'): toavah (H8441, abomination/detestable thing) — Ezekiel's other characteristic word for religious betrayal, alongside gillulim.
5: vaevachar beyisrael ('I chose Israel'): bachar (H977, choose/select) — the divine election vocabulary. vaessa yadi ('I raised my hand/swore'): the oath gesture — raising the hand is the physical accompaniment of the most solemn oath. YHWH's choosing of Israel is grounded in sovereign grace, not Israel's merit (Deuteronomy 7:6-8). lezara beit-yaaqov ('to the seed/offspring of the house of Jacob'): the election reaches back to the patriarchal promise.
6: eretz zavat chalab udvash ('a land flowing with milk and honey'): the Exodus-formula for the Promised Land (Exodus 3:8,17; Leviticus 20:24; Numbers 14:8; Deuteronomy 6:3 etc.) — occurring 20× in the Pentateuch. In Ezekiel's retrospective it becomes evidence of YHWH's gracious intent: the gift was always the goal. tzvi hi lechol haaratzot ('the most glorious/the glory of all lands'): tzvi (H6643, glory/beauty/gazelle) — the land as the beautiful prize, the destination of YHWH's desire for his people.
7: ish et-shiqutzei einav hashlichu ('each of you must cast away the detestable things of his eyes'): shiqutzim (H8251, detestable things/abominations) — idols described by what they do to the senses: they are things the eyes desire (einav). vebegillulei mitzraim al-titammeu ('do not defile yourselves with Egypt's idols'): the idolatry Israel must leave behind in Egypt is already in their hearts. The Exodus is as much an inner detachment from Egypt as a geographic departure.
8: vayamru lo nishma elecha ('but they rebelled against me and would not listen'): marah (H4784, rebel/be contentious) — the first rebellion, in Egypt itself, before the Exodus. The failure to cast away Egyptian idols (Ezekiel 20:7) was not ignorance but refusal. eshpoch chamati ('I said I would pour out my fury'): shaphak (H8210, pour out) + chemah (H2534, fury/heat) — the covenant anger that was consistently restrained throughout the history.
9: vaase lema'an shemi ('but I acted for the sake of my name'): the theological pivot that recurs four times in this chapter (Ezekiel 20:9,14,22,44). shem (H8034, name) — YHWH's name is his reputation, his identity-in-relationship, his covenant self-disclosure. To act 'for the sake of my name' is to maintain covenant integrity even when the covenant partner has forfeited it. lehallel ('to be profaned'): chalal (H2490, profane/defile/pierce) — the Niphal: YHWH's name would be profaned if he destroyed Israel before the watching nations, for they would conclude his power was limited.
10: vaotzi otam me-eretz mitzraim ('so I brought them out of Egypt'): yatsa (H3318, go out/bring out) in the Hiphil — the Exodus verb. The word appears throughout the chapter (Ezekiel 20:6,9,10,14,22,34,38,41,42) as the theological backbone: YHWH's primary redemptive act is the bringing-out, and this chapter recasts it as the beginning of a pattern that will be repeated in the new exodus from Babylon.
11: vaeten lahem et-chuqqotai ('I gave them my statutes'): choq (H2706, statute/engraving) — the word for law as something engraved or incised, fixed and permanent. Alongside mishpatim (H4941, ordinances/judgments — from shaphat, to judge). asher yaaseh otam haadam vachai bahem ('which if a person does them, he will live by them'): the Leviticus 18:5 citation — the principle that the law contains life within it; doing the law is the path to life. Paul cites this in Galatians 3:12 and Romans 10:5 to contrast with the righteousness of faith.
12: vegam et-shabbetotai natati lahem ('I also gave them my sabbaths'): shabbat (H7676, sabbath/rest) — the gift vocabulary is striking: the sabbath is not a burden imposed but a grace bestowed. lihyot leot beni uveinam ('as a sign between me and them'): ot (H226, sign/token/signal/wonder) — the sabbath as the distinguishing mark of the covenant, the visible indicator of the YHWH-Israel relationship in time (Exodus 31:13-17). ledet ki ani YHWH meqaddisham ('so they would know that I am YHWH who sanctifies them'): qadash (H6942, be holy/sanctify/set apart) — YHWH as the sanctifier; the sabbath is the temporal enactment of Israel's holiness.
13: vayimredu vi beit yisrael ('but the house of Israel rebelled against me'): the rebellion in the wilderness — the Sinai-to-Kadesh generation. veet-shabbetotai chilelu mead ('they profaned my sabbaths greatly'): chalal (H2490, profane) + meod (greatly/exceedingly) — the sabbath violation as particularly significant because the sabbath is the sign of the covenant; profaning it attacks the visible token of the relationship itself.
14: vaase lema'an shemi ('but I acted for the sake of my name'): second occurrence of the name-protection motif. The restraint of the justified divine wrath 'for the sake of the name' is the OT foundation for Paul's theology of divine forbearance in Romans 3:25-26 — God passing over former sins during the period before the cross.
15: vegam ani nasati yadi lahem bamidbar ('but I also swore in the wilderness'): the second oath, negative — not to bring them into the land. The land that YHWH described positively in Ezekiel 20:6 (tzvi... zavat chalab udvash) is now withheld from the rebellious generation. The greatness of the gift makes the withholding more pointed.
16: ki bemishpatai maasu ('because they despised my ordinances'): maas (H3988, despise/reject/refuse) — the active rejection of the covenant's defining content. velibam holech acharei gilluleihem ('their hearts were set after/followed their idols'): lev (H3820, heart) — the directing of the inner person after gillulim (H1544, idols/dung-pellets). The heart going after idols is the covenant betrayal that explains every external violation.
17: vatachos eini alehem mishachet ('nevertheless my eye had pity on them, not destroying them'): chus (H2347, spare/have compassion on) — the unexpected mercy that interrupts the pattern of rebellion-judgment. YHWH's pity is not earned; it appears as a grace-moment within the judgment narrative. This recurs throughout the chapter as the divine counter-pattern to human unfaithfulness.
18: vaomar el-beneihem bamidbar ('and I said to their children in the wilderness'): the second generation receives direct instruction after the first generation is barred from the land. The instruction begins with negative commands: behuqqot avoteichem al-telechu ('do not follow your fathers' statutes') — the children are warned against inheriting their parents' patterns, not against the covenant itself.
19: ani YHWH eloheichem ('I am YHWH your God'): the Decalogue's opening formula (Exodus 20:2; Deuteronomy 5:6) — the covenant identity declaration that grounds all the following commands. bechukotai telechu ('walk in my statutes'): halak (H1980, walk) as the metaphor for conduct — life as movement in a direction, with YHWH's statutes as the path.
20: et-shabbetotai qaddoshu ('keep my sabbaths holy'): qadash in Piel (H6942, to sanctify/treat as holy) — the human responsibility to treat the sabbath as the holy thing YHWH has made it. vehayo leot beni uveinechem ('they will be a sign between me and you'): the sabbath as the covenant-identity marker appears in this chapter's context as the single most important distinguishing practice — its observance or neglect becomes the synecdoche for covenant fidelity or betrayal.
21: The second generation rebels as the first did — the structural repetition shows the depth of the problem. Neither the punishment of the first generation nor the explicit instruction (Ezekiel 20:18-20) breaks the cycle. veshabbetotai chilelu ('they profaned my sabbaths'): the sabbath-profanation recurs. The pattern cannot be broken by law, instruction, or punishment — which sets up the new covenant promise of Ezekiel 36:26-27 where YHWH says he will act internally rather than externally.
22: vaasef et-yadi ('but I withdrew my hand'): the restraint of divine judgment — 'pulling back' the hand raised for punishment. Third occurrence of the name-protection motif. YHWH's consistency in holding back judgment 'for the sake of my name' across three generations reveals the patient divine pedagogy: the repeated offer of repentance, the repeated restraint of deserved judgment.
23: gam ani nasati et-yadi lahem bamidbar lehafitz otam bagoyim ('but I also swore to scatter them among the nations'): the counter-oath — the promise of judgment-through-scattering that Leviticus 26:33 and Deuteronomy 4:27; 28:64 established. The dispersion is not an accident of history but a covenant consequence. The exile is therefore not YHWH's abandonment of his people but the execution of a covenantal clause that Israel itself agreed to (Deut 27-28).
24: veyaen bemishpatai lo asu ('because they had not carried out my ordinances'): the three-part indictment — rejected mishpatim, despised huqqim, profaned shabbatot, and hearts after fathers' idols. The sabbath-profanation appears again as the lead indicator: to violate the sign is to dissolve the relationship it signifies.
25: vegam ani natati lahem huqqim lo tovim ('I also gave them statutes that were not good'): this troubling verse refers to YHWH giving over Israel to the consequences of their own rebellious choices — 'statutes not good' meaning the idolatrous practices they already followed, which YHWH 'gave' them by withdrawing his restraining hand. The 'statutes by which one cannot live' are the self-destructive practices of the nations (cf. Romans 1:24,26,28: God 'gave them over' to their desires).
26: vatimme otam bematnotam ('I made them unclean through their own offerings'): the defilement by one's own offerings — particularly child sacrifice (behaavir kol-peter rechem, 'in causing every firstborn to pass through the fire'). This is the bitter irony: what they intended as worship YHWH counted as self-contamination. lema'an ashem ('so that they might be desolate/appalled'): shamem (H8074, be desolate/appalled) — the horror of seeing one's own actions lead to desolation.
27: uven bigedu vi avoteichem ('your fathers committed treachery against me'): baged (H898, act treacherously/faithlessly) — the covenant betrayal vocabulary from the marriage metaphors of Hosea and Jeremiah. maalo maalu vi ('they committed unfaithfulness against me'): maal (H4603, act treacherously/commit trespass) — the doubling intensifies: persistent, habitual covenant unfaithfulness.
28: vaaviEM el-haaretz ('when I brought them into the land'): the transition from wilderness to settlement — the land-gift that should have ended the cycle. Instead: kol-givah ramah ('every high hill') and kol-etz avot ('every leafy tree') become sites of sacrifice. The land that was to be the locus of pure YHWH-worship becomes instead the expansion of Canaanite high-place practice.
29: mah-habamah asher atem habaim sham ('what is this high place to which you go?'): YHWH's question about the bamah — the word-play on bama (H1116, high place) may be connected to ba (H935, come/go) to suggest 'the place-you-go-to.' The name Bamah sticks ('its name has been called Bamah to this day') as an indictment: the illicit worship site has become a named landmark of disobedience.
30: hagedrekei avoteichem atem nitmaim ('are you making yourselves unclean in the same way as your fathers?'): the rhetorical question with a known answer. acharei shiqutzeihem atem zonim ('do you go whoring after their detestable things?'): zanah (H2181, whore/commit fornication/be unfaithful) — the marriage-covenant vocabulary: idolatry as adultery. This connects to Ezekiel's extended use of sexual-unfaithfulness imagery for religious betrayal (Ezekiel 16, 23).
31: uvehaniash mattanotechem ('when you present your gifts'): the child-sacrifice language recurs (behaavir benaichem baesh). im-yiddaresh ani lachem ('I will not be consulted by you'): the refusal to respond to hypocritical inquiry, as in Ezekiel 20:3. The chapter's frame: the elders came to inquire; YHWH refuses; he instead reviews their history to explain why the consultation is invalid.
32: vehaaolah al-ruchechem hayoh lo tihyeh ('what you are thinking will not happen'): ruach (H7307, spirit/breath/wind) here used for 'thought/mind.' The people's desire to be like the nations (kegoyim) and worship wood and stone is a direct repudiation of Israel's covenant calling (Deuteronomy 4:5-8; Ezekiel 14:2). YHWH refuses this self-assimilation: the calling cannot be discarded.
33: chay ani — the divine oath. beyad chazaqah uvizroa netuyah ('with a mighty hand and outstretched arm'): the Exodus formula (Deuteronomy 4:34; Ezekiel 5:15; 7:19 etc.) — the most recognizable shorthand for YHWH's redemptive power. The same mighty hand and outstretched arm that brought Israel out of Egypt will govern their return from exile. uvecheimah shefuchah ('and with outpoured fury'): the return will not be without judgment; the new exodus passes through a wilderness of accountability before reaching restoration.
34: vehozeti etchem min-haammim ('I will bring you out from the peoples'): yatsa (H3318) again — the bringing-out verb of the Exodus applied to the return from exile. The new exodus pattern is explicit: the same mighty hand, the same bringing-out, the same journey toward the covenant relationship — but after a severe discipline.
35: vahaveti etchem el-midbar haamim ('I will bring you into the wilderness of the peoples'): the new exodus will include a new wilderness — a place of divine confrontation before the land is entered. venispot etchem sham panim el-panim ('and there I will contend with you face to face'): panim el-panim — the direct personal confrontation (cf. Genesis 32:30, Jacob wrestling with God face to face). The wilderness of peoples is a space for the covenant lawsuit to be prosecuted personally.
36: kaasher nispot et-avoteichem bemidbar eretz mitzraim ('as I contended with your fathers in the wilderness of Egypt'): the wilderness of the nations will replicate the Sinai wilderness experience — a second giving of the covenant. The new exodus recapitulates the first; the God who acted then will act again in the same pattern.
37: vehaaveti etchem tachat hashevet ('I will cause you to pass under the shepherd's rod'): shevet (H7626, rod/staff/tribe/scepter) — the shepherd's counting-rod. In ancient practice, sheep passed under the rod one by one for counting and inspection (Leviticus 27:32). uveheveti bemasoret habrit ('and I will bring you into the bond/chain of the covenant'): massoret (H4562, chain/bond) — the covenant as a binding obligation. The image is both protective (flock under the shepherd's care) and obligating (the covenant that binds both parties).
38: uvaroti mikem hamordim vehamopeshim bi ('I will purge out from among you the rebels and those who sin against me'): barar (H1305, purge/purify/select) — the purging of the dross from the refined metal; the same term appears in Malachi 3:3 (the refiner's fire). The return is selective: the rebels will be removed before the restored community enters the land. The exodus-through-the-wilderness produces a purified remnant.
39: The bitter irony of YHWH's concession: if you will not listen, at least stop defiling his name with your gillulim. The minimum demand — if you cannot stop idolatry, at least do not mix it with YHWH-worship. This is not approval of idolatry but the final statement before the positive turn of Ezekiel 20:40-44: half-hearted worship is worse than straightforward defection because it pollutes the name.
40: bebhar qodshi ('on my holy mountain'): the eschatological gathering point. sham yaaveduni kol-beit yisrael kuloh ('there all the house of Israel — all of it — will serve me'): the restored worship is complete-Israel worship: no division, no partial remnant — kuloh ('all of it') — the healing of the division between north and south, the ingathering of all the dispersed. sham eratzehem ('there I will accept them'): ratzah (H7521, be pleased with/accept/satisfy a debt) — divine acceptance of the restored offering.
41: berei'ach nichoach ertzechem ('I will accept you as a pleasing aroma'): rei'ach nichoach (H7381 + H5207) — the 'pleasing aroma' of acceptable sacrifice (Genesis 8:21; Exodus 29:18,25,41; Leviticus 1:9,13,17 etc.) — the technical term for what makes an offering acceptable to YHWH. The restored people are themselves the pleasing sacrifice. venikdashti bachem leeinei hagoyim ('I will show my holiness through you before the nations'): the priestly-national vocation of Israel (Exodus 19:5-6) is fulfilled in the restoration: YHWH is sanctified before the nations through the visible restoration of his people.
42: vidate'u ki ani YHWH ('you will know that I am YHWH'): the recognition formula that runs through Ezekiel — in exile the Israelites will know YHWH because of the judgment; in restoration they will know him because of the redemption. The same divine Name reveals itself through both modes of action.
43: ushantechem sham et-darkechem ('there you will remember your ways'): zakar (H2142, remember) — the memory of covenant failure is a gift, not a burden: it contextualizes the grace of restoration. veniqottem bifinechem ('you will loathe yourselves'): qot (H6962, loathe/feel disgust) — the repentance that comes not from fear of punishment but from seeing one's own past against the backdrop of received grace. This is the repentance the restored community will experience after the new exodus.
44: vide'etem ki ani YHWH baasoti itchem lema'an shemi ('you will know that I am YHWH when I have dealt with you for my name's sake'): the fourth occurrence of the name-protection motif. The chapter closes with the same divine logic that has governed it throughout: YHWH acts consistently for the sake of his name/character/covenant integrity. velo kederacheichem haraim ('and not according to your evil ways'): the restoration is entirely YHWH's initiative, not earned by the people's improved behavior. The new covenant operates from grace, not merit.
45: New oracle formula: the word of YHWH turns to a brief southern-fire oracle (Ezekiel 20:45-49 in English numbering). This short oracle serves as a transitional appendix to Ezekiel 20 and a prelude to the sword oracles of Ezekiel 21.
46: pane darkecha teiman ('set your face toward the south'): teiman (H8486, south/Negev) — the southern direction toward Jerusalem and Judah from Ezekiel's Babylonian location. hatteph el-derech negev ('speak/drip toward the south'): nataph (H5197, drip/drop/prophesy) — used for prophetic utterance that falls like rain-drops (Amos 7:16; Micah 2:6,11). The image of prophecy as dripping/dropping carries the sense of measured, accumulating utterance.
47: vehitzi baesh ('I will kindle a fire in you'): the forest-fire oracle — ya'ar hanegev (the forest of the Negev) is an unusual designation, since the Negev is characteristically treeless desert; the forest imagery may be allegorical for Jerusalem's population (cf. Ezekiel 21 where the 'forest' becomes the sword oracle against Judah). kol-panim mineggev tzaphona ('every face from south to north'): comprehensive — the fire leaves no face unscorched, corresponding to the comprehensive sword-judgment to follow.
48: verayu kol-basar ki ani YHWH bilati oto ('all flesh will see that I the LORD have lit this fire; it will not be quenched'): the universal witness formula. The unquenchable fire is the inescapable divine judgment; the prophet's word sets it; YHWH's name ensures it cannot be extinguished by human countermeasures.
49: va-aMaR ah adonai YHWH hemah omerim li ('then I said: Ah, Lord GOD! They are saying of me'): Ezekiel's complaint — his hearers dismiss the oracle as merely parabolic (moshel meshalim hu, 'he is a teller of parables'). The prophet is accused of speaking in riddles rather than plain prophecy. This reaction is itself a fulfillment of Ezekiel 33:31-32: hearing words but not acting on them, treating prophecy as entertainment. The chapter that began with YHWH refusing to be consulted ends with the prophet being dismissed as a parable-teller.
